title: Spinning cotton on a supported spindle
subtitle: Drafting cotton fibres on a takli or tahkli spindle using the short-draw supported technique
excerpt: Cotton fibres are very short (1.5-3.5 cm) compared to wool and require a quite different approach: a lightweight supported spindle that rests in a small bowl rather than hanging free, a very short draft zone, and minimal twist per length. The takli or tahkli is the standard tool. This guide covers the setup, the cotton short-draw, and the difference between cotton and wool spinning mechanics.
sourceNotes: Pellew, Charles N. 'Dyes and Dyeing.' McBride, Nast, 1913. Covers cotton fibre properties and preparation (Chapter 2). Internet Archive: archive.org/details/dyesdyeing00pellrich. Method for supported-spindle cotton spinning is observation-led and consistent with traditional South Asian takli practice.

content.text: or a similarly lightweight supported spindle. Drop spindles are too heavy and fast for cotton's short staple; the fibres snap before they can form yarn. The
keeps the draft zone short enough that the 1.5-2 cm fibres remain under control throughout.

items.name: Cotton top or combed sliver (not roving: cotton sliver is combed differently)
Small smooth bowl or tile (spindle support)

content.text: Pull a short section of cotton sliver, about 15 cm, from the bundle. Cotton sliver is soft and very loosely held together. Do not pull it into thin wisps as you would with wool; cotton needs to be drafted directly from the sliver with the short-draw motion.

Rest the spindle shaft in the bowl. Roll the shaft between your thumb and index finger of the dominant hand to set it spinning. The spindle should spin freely in the bowl for several seconds on a good flick.

With the non-dominant hand, hold the cotton sliver about 3-4 cm above the forming yarn. The dominant hand holds the spindle. Pinch very close to the last cm of formed yarn and draw the non-dominant hand back 1-2 cm only, releasing tension so twist runs forward into the drafted section. Do not draft more than 2 cm at a time: the fibres will disengage and the yarn will break. Flick the spindle again to add more twist before the next short draft.

When 10-15 cm of yarn has formed, wind it onto the spindle shaft and resume. Cotton yarn winds more tightly than wool; keep the angle of wind steep to avoid the yarn slipping. Wind off onto the niddy-noddy when the spindle is full. Skein and tie as for wool.

items.fix: Flick the spindle more often and draw back only 1 cm at a time. If breaks continue, add more twist before each draft.
items.cause: Draft zone too long for the short cotton staple, or too little twist before drafting
items.problem: The yarn breaks constantly during drafting

items.fix: Establish a rhythm: one flick, one 1-cm draft, repeat. The rhythm is slower than wool spinning.
items.cause: Inconsistent short-draw: varying how far the fibres are drafted each time
items.problem: The yarn is lumpy with thick and thin sections

items.fix: Use a smaller bowl with a slight concavity, or a smooth flat tile with a small hollow.
items.cause: Bowl too large or too slippery
items.problem: The spindle slips sideways in the bowl
